walkthrough.title: Sort the Building Tiles
walkthrough.text: Let's start by organizing all the building tiles into six color-coded types: blue Pavilions, red Seraglios, brown Arcades, white Chambers, green Gardens, and purple Towers. Sort them face-down into separate stacks by their back color. Each tile shows a building illustration, a purchase price, and walls on zero to three edges.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Building Market
walkthrough.text: Let's set up the building market board in the center of the table with its four spaces, one for each currency type: Denars, Dirham, Ducats, and Gulden. Draw one building tile randomly and place it face-up on each of the four market spaces. These are the first four buildings available for purchase.

walkthrough.title: Create the Bag of Tiles
walkthrough.text: Let's mix all the remaining building tiles together face-down and place them in the cloth bag. Throughout the game, tiles will be drawn from this bag to refill the market. Give the bag a good shake to make sure the tiles are thoroughly mixed.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Money Cards
walkthrough.text: Let's prepare the money cards, which come in four currencies with values ranging from one to nine: Denars, Dirham, Ducats, and Gulden. Shuffle all the money cards together into one face-down draw deck, then deal four cards face-up into a display next to it. This display is the money market where players will collect currency.

walkthrough.title: Deal Starting Money
walkthrough.text: Let's deal starting money to each player by dealing cards face-down from the deck until each player has a total value of at least twenty. Players look at their cards but keep them hidden from opponents. Any leftover value beyond twenty is fine to keep.

walkthrough.title: Distribute Starting Fountains
walkthrough.text: Let's give each player their starting fountain tile, which is the center of your Alhambra. Every tile you place must connect back to this fountain through open edges. Place your fountain in front of you with space to build outward in all directions.

walkthrough.title: Set Up Scoring Round Cards
walkthrough.text: Let's shuffle the two scoring round cards into the money deck by dividing the deck into five roughly equal piles. Shuffle the first scoring card into the second pile and the second scoring card into the fourth pile, then stack all five piles back together in order. These cards will trigger scoring rounds at unpredictable moments during the game.

walkthrough.title: Prepare Your Reserve Board
walkthrough.text: Let's make sure each player has space next to their Alhambra for a reserve, which is a personal holding area for tiles you buy but do not want to place immediately. Tiles in the reserve do not score, but they are available for placement on future turns. You are now ready to begin building your Alhambra.

Sort face-down by color into separate stacks

Place 1 random face-up tile on each space

summary.title: Prepare Your Reserve Board

Tiles stored here don't score but are available for later placement

walkthrough.title: Turn Overview — Three Actions
walkthrough.text: Let's learn the three actions you can take on your turn: take money, buy a building tile, or redesign your Alhambra. On each turn, you choose exactly one of these actions. The one exception is the exact change bonus, which we will cover shortly.

walkthrough.title: Action One — Take Money
walkthrough.text: Let's start with the simplest action: you may take one money card of any value from the face-up display, or take multiple cards as long as their total value is five or less. After taking cards, refill the display from the deck. This is how you build your purchasing power for future turns.

walkthrough.title: Action Two — Buy a Building Tile
walkthrough.text: Let's look at how buying works: choose one tile from the building market and pay its price using money cards that match the currency of the market space the tile sits on. You must pay at least the tile's printed price, and there is no change given in Alhambra. Any overpayment is permanently lost.

walkthrough.title: The Exact Change Bonus
walkthrough.text: Let's talk about the most important rule in Alhambra: if you pay the exact price for a tile with no overpayment, you immediately get another full turn. This bonus turn can chain, so paying exact change twice in a row gives you three total turns. Collecting the right denominations of each currency is critical to triggering this powerful bonus.

walkthrough.title: Placing a Purchased Tile
walkthrough.text: Let's learn tile placement: after buying a tile, you must either place it into your Alhambra or put it in your reserve. When placing, the tile must connect to your existing Alhambra so that walls align with walls and open edges align with open edges. Every tile must be reachable from your fountain by walking through open edges only.

walkthrough.title: Wall Placement Rules
walkthrough.text: Let's examine wall rules carefully: walls are the thick dark lines on tile edges, and when you place a tile, its walls must touch other walls or face outward toward empty space. You can never place a tile so a wall faces an open edge of an adjacent tile. You must also always be able to reach every tile in your Alhambra from the fountain through connected open edges.

walkthrough.title: Action Three — Redesign Your Alhambra
walkthrough.text: Let's cover the redesign action, which lets you do exactly one of three things: remove a tile from your Alhambra to your reserve, take a tile from your reserve and place it into your Alhambra, or swap a tile between the two. All placement rules still apply after the redesign. This action costs your whole turn but is invaluable for fixing mistakes or optimizing your layout.

walkthrough.title: The Reserve
walkthrough.text: Let's understand the reserve, your personal holding area for tiles you own but have not placed. Tiles in the reserve do not count toward scoring for building majorities or for your longest wall. The reserve is useful when you buy a tile that does not fit your current layout or when you need to rearrange your Alhambra later.

walkthrough.title: Scoring Round One
walkthrough.text: Let's look at how the first scoring round works: when the first scoring card is drawn from the money deck, scoring happens immediately for each of the six building types. In the first round, only the player in first place for each type scores points. Ties are broken by dividing points among tied players, rounded down.

walkthrough.title: Scoring Round Two
walkthrough.text: Let's move to the second scoring round, triggered when the second scoring card appears. Now both first place and second place score for each building type, with first place earning higher points. Having even one tile of a type nobody else collected can earn you easy second-place points.

walkthrough.title: Final Scoring — Round Three
walkthrough.text: Let's cover the final scoring at game end: when the bag runs out of tiles and the market cannot be fully refilled, the game ends and a third scoring round occurs. This time, first, second, and third place all score for each building type with the highest point values of the entire game. The final round is worth the most points overall.

walkthrough.title: Building Type Scoring Values
walkthrough.text: Let's review the first-place point values for each building type across all three scoring rounds: Pavilions score one, eight, and sixteen; Seraglios score two, nine, and seventeen; Arcades score three, ten, and eighteen; Chambers score four, eleven, and nineteen; Gardens score five, twelve, and twenty; and Towers score six, thirteen, and twenty-one. Higher-value buildings are rarer, so the competition is fiercer. Second and third place earn the lower values from this same progression.

walkthrough.title: Longest Wall Bonus
walkthrough.text: Let's learn about the wall bonus: at each scoring round, every player counts the length of their longest continuous external wall around their Alhambra in wall segments along the outside perimeter. Each player scores points equal to their longest wall length. Walls matter at every scoring round, so plan your tile placement to extend one continuous perimeter.

walkthrough.title: Game End and Final Tally
walkthrough.text: Let's wrap up the rules: the game ends when the building market cannot be completely refilled from the bag. Perform the final scoring round, add wall bonuses, and total all points from all three rounds to determine the winner. In case of a tie, the player with the most money cards remaining wins.

bullets: Choose 1 action per turn: take money, buy a tile, or redesign
Exception: exact change → bonus turn

walkthrough.title: Welcome and Meet the Players
walkthrough.text: Let's walk through a scripted practice game of Alhambra with four players: Player one focuses on Gardens and Pavilions, Player two targets Towers and Seraglios, Player three is our patient strategist, and Player four is going to make some very instructive mistakes. Follow along even if your group is a different size. Player four will teach us what not to do.

walkthrough.title: The Starting Market and First Turns
walkthrough.text: Let's look at the initial market: a Pavilion at three Denars, a Tower at ten Dirham, an Arcade at six Ducats, and a Garden at four Gulden. Player one takes two low-value money cards totaling three to build currency variety, Player two pays exactly ten Dirham for the Tower and earns a bonus turn, and Player three takes a high-value Ducats card to build a war chest for later.

walkthrough.title: Player Four — Mistake One: Overpaying
walkthrough.text: Let's see the first mistake: Player four wants the Arcade priced at six Ducats but only has cards worth three, three, and five. Instead of waiting to collect exact change, Player four pays three plus five for a total of eight, overpaying by two. That is two Ducats of value thrown away and no bonus turn earned.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Collect Exact Change Before Buying
walkthrough.text: Let's understand why overpaying hurts so much: there is no change given in Alhambra, so every coin you overpay is permanently lost and you miss the exact-change bonus turn. Over the course of a game, a player who overpays three or four times will fall behind by the equivalent of three or four full turns. Spend a turn or two collecting money cards in the right denominations before buying.

walkthrough.title: Player One Buys with Exact Change
walkthrough.text: Let's see smart purchasing in action: the market refills with a Seraglio at seven Dirham and a Chamber at five Ducats, and Player one has carefully assembled Gulden cards totaling exactly four. They buy the Garden for exactly four Gulden, earn a bonus turn, and use it to take two low-value money cards totaling under five. That is two actions in one turn, all from planning denominations.

walkthrough.title: Player Four — Mistake Two: Walls Facing Inward
walkthrough.text: Let's watch Player four's second mistake: they place their Arcade tile next to the fountain with walls facing inward toward the center of their Alhambra. This creates a dead end because future tiles cannot connect through wall edges. Player four has boxed themselves in, limiting where they can place tiles going forward.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Keep Walls on the Outside
walkthrough.text: Let's learn the wall placement principle: walls should generally face outward toward the perimeter of your Alhambra, because walls facing inward block expansion paths and create unreachable dead zones. Before placing a tile, count how many open edges remain for future connections. If a placement chokes off growth, use your reserve to hold the tile until a better position opens up.

walkthrough.title: Player Two Builds a Smart Wall
walkthrough.text: Let's see Player two build smartly by placing their Tower tile on the outer edge with two walls facing outward along the perimeter. This adds two segments to their longest wall while keeping the inner connections open for future tiles. Player two is thinking about both building majorities and wall bonus points simultaneously.

walkthrough.title: First Scoring Round Arrives
walkthrough.text: Let's see scoring in action: the first scoring card is drawn and only first place scores for each building type. Player one leads in Gardens with two tiles and scores five points, while Player two leads in Towers with one tile and scores six points. Players three and four have no majorities and score zero for buildings.

walkthrough.title: Player Four — Mistake Three: Spreading Too Thin
walkthrough.text: Let's examine Player four's collection: one Pavilion, one Seraglio, one Arcade, one Chamber, one Garden, and one Tower. One tile of every type looks impressive but scores terribly because Player four had zero majorities at the first scoring round. Having one of everything means first place in nothing.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Focus on Two or Three Building Types
walkthrough.text: Let's understand majority strategy: scoring rewards the players with the most tiles of each type, not the most variety. Pick two or three building types to focus on and buy aggressively in those categories while letting opponents fight over the rest. A player with four Gardens and three Chambers will massively outscore a player with one of everything.

walkthrough.title: Player Three Chains Bonus Turns
walkthrough.text: Let's watch Player three's patient strategy pay off: after collecting money for several turns, they have exact change for two different market tiles. They buy one tile with exact change, earn a bonus turn, then buy a second tile with exact change and earn yet another bonus turn. Three actions in one turn sequence is the explosive payoff of building the right denominations.

walkthrough.title: Player Four Learns — Focus and Reserve
walkthrough.text: Let's see Player four adjust by focusing on Chambers and Arcades only, buying a Chamber tile that does not fit their current layout. Instead of forcing a bad placement, they put the tile in reserve and use the redesign action next turn to swap it with a poorly placed tile. Smart use of the reserve fixes earlier wall mistakes.

walkthrough.title: Second Scoring Round
walkthrough.text: Let's tally the second scoring round where first and second place both score: Player one leads Gardens at twelve points, Player two leads Towers at thirteen points, and Player three leads Arcades and Seraglios. Player four managed second place in Chambers for eleven points thanks to their mid-game focus shift. Wall bonuses are counted again with Player two at eight segments.

walkthrough.title: End Game and Final Scoring
walkthrough.text: Let's see the final turns play out as the bag runs low and players make last purchases to secure majority positions. Player three uses a redesign turn to extend their longest wall by three segments, and Player four finally pays exact change for a Chamber. In the third scoring round, first, second, and third place all score with the highest values of the game.

walkthrough.title: Final Results
walkthrough.text: Let's count the final scores: Player two wins with strong Tower and Seraglio majorities plus the longest wall, while Player three finishes close behind with Arcade dominance. Player one scores well on Gardens, and Player four finishes last but scored far better in rounds two and three than in round one thanks to adjusting strategy mid-game.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: Congratulations on completing your first practice game of Alhambra! You have seen how exact change earns bonus turns, how wall placement affects future expansion, and why focusing on two or three building types beats spreading thin. Player four made every common mistake and then showed how to recover.

walkthrough.title: Before You Build
walkthrough.text:
Before you start a full game, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for core strategy principles and the most common mistakes to avoid.

Second, reference the Appendix for a quick guide to building types, currencies, scoring values, and game terms.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Now go build the most magnificent Alhambra at the table.

summary.title: Before You Build
bullets: Check out General Tips → core strategy principles
Reference the Appendix → building types, currencies, scoring values
Ask your personal GM Guide → tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Exact Change Is Everything
walkthrough.text: Let's talk about the most powerful mechanic in Alhambra: paying exact change for a building tile gives you an immediate bonus turn, and bonus turns can chain. A player who pays exact change three times in a game gets three extra turns over someone who always overpays. Spend turns collecting the right denominations before buying rather than overpaying to grab a tile now.

walkthrough.title: Focus on Two or Three Building Types
walkthrough.text: Let's avoid the most common beginner trap: scoring rewards majorities in each building type, not variety. A player with four Gardens scores the Garden bonus every round, while a player with one of each type scores nothing. Pick two or three types to collect aggressively and ignore the rest unless an easy majority presents itself.

walkthrough.title: Plan Your Walls Outward
walkthrough.text: Let's think about wall placement before you lock in a tile: walls should generally face outward along your Alhambra's perimeter because inward-facing walls block expansion paths and create unreachable dead zones. Before placing a tile, count how many open connection points remain. If a placement closes off future growth, use your reserve instead.

walkthrough.title: Use Your Reserve Strategically
walkthrough.text: Let's appreciate the reserve, because new players often forget it exists. If you buy a tile that does not fit your current layout, store it in reserve and use the redesign action on a future turn to swap it in when the time is right. The reserve lets you buy tiles when the price is right without being forced into bad placements.

walkthrough.title: Watch What Opponents Collect
walkthrough.text: Let's track the competition: if two opponents are both collecting Towers, let them fight over that majority and focus your energy on types with less competition. The building market is shared, so when you see a tile that would help an opponent clinch a majority, consider buying it defensively even if you do not need it. Denying a key tile can be worth more than the tile itself.

walkthrough.title: Longest Wall Is Free Points
walkthrough.text: Let's not forget the wall bonus: at every scoring round, each player scores points equal to their longest continuous external wall, and this bonus often decides close games. When choosing between two otherwise equal tile placements, pick the one that extends your longest wall. Tiles with two or three wall edges placed on the perimeter are wall-bonus gold.

walkthrough.title: Take Money When Nothing Good Is Available
walkthrough.text: Let's be patient, because not every turn needs to be a purchase. If the building market has nothing you want or you lack exact change, take money cards instead, grabbing two or three low-value cards that total five or less to build your exact-change options. A turn spent taking money is never wasted if it sets up an exact-change purchase next turn.

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Let's list the traps that cost beginners the most points: overpaying repeatedly instead of collecting exact change, spreading purchases across all six building types instead of focusing on two or three, and placing walls inward to block future expansion. Two more mistakes to watch for are forgetting to use the reserve for awkward tiles and ignoring the wall bonus entirely. Avoid these five mistakes and you will finish in the top half of every game.

3 exact-change buys = 3 extra turns over someone who always overpays
Spend turns collecting right denominations before buying

summary.title: Take Money When Nothing Good Is Available

summary.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
bullets: Overpaying instead of collecting exact change
Spreading across all 6 types instead of focusing on 2-3
Placing walls inward, blocking future expansion
Forgetting the reserve exists
Ignoring the wall bonus entirely

walkthrough.title: Denomination Management
walkthrough.text: Let's master the currency game within the game: the key to chaining bonus turns is maintaining a portfolio of money cards across all four currencies in small denominations. Cards valued one through four are the most flexible because they combine to hit exact prices more often. When the money display shows low-value cards, snap them up even if you do not need that currency immediately.

walkthrough.title: Defensive Tile Purchasing
walkthrough.text: Let's talk about buying tiles you do not need: if an opponent is one Tower away from clinching the Tower majority worth thirteen points in round two, buying that Tower yourself is worth more than almost anything else on the market. Store it in your reserve if you cannot place it. The cost of one turn and some money is far less than the thirteen points your opponent would have scored.

walkthrough.title: Wall Optimization and Perimeter Planning
walkthrough.text: Let's think about walls as a scoring engine: advanced players build their Alhambra in an L-shape or U-shape to maximize the outer perimeter, since a compact square has a shorter wall than a sprawling irregular shape. Place high-wall tiles on the outer edges and low-wall or no-wall tiles in the interior. The wall bonus at each scoring round can be worth eight to twelve points for a well-planned perimeter.

walkthrough.title: Scoring Round Timing
walkthrough.text: Let's anticipate the scoring cards: the first scoring card is in the second fifth of the deck and the second is in the fourth fifth, so as the deck thins you can estimate when scoring is approaching. Before a scoring round, prioritize placing tiles from your reserve into your Alhambra so they count toward majorities. If you are one tile behind an opponent, the turn before scoring is the time to buy aggressively.

walkthrough.title: High-Value Building Type Strategy
walkthrough.text: Let's evaluate which building types to target: Towers score six, thirteen, and twenty-one across the three rounds for first place, while Pavilions score just one, eight, and sixteen. The sweet spot is often Gardens or Chambers in the middle range where the scoring is solid but competition is lower. Read the table and pick the types that fewest opponents are pursuing.

walkthrough.title: The Reserve as a Rearrangement Tool
walkthrough.text: Let's use the reserve for late-game optimization: your Alhambra may have tiles placed early that now block better configurations. Use the redesign action to pull out a poorly placed tile and swap in one from reserve that extends your wall or fills a gap. One well-timed redesign can add three or four wall segments to your longest wall.

walkthrough.title: Reading the Money Display
walkthrough.text: Let's extract information from the money display: when it refills, it tells you something about what currencies remain in the deck, and if you keep seeing high-value Dirham cards the remaining Dirham may skew low. Also watch what opponents take from the display, because if someone is hoarding Ducats they are likely targeting a tile in the Ducats market slot. Information advantage enables better purchasing decisions.

walkthrough.title: Endgame Point Maximization
walkthrough.text: Let's plan the final turns precisely: when the bag is nearly empty, count tiles remaining and estimate how many turns you have left. Prioritize actions in this order: place reserve tiles that secure majorities, buy tiles with exact change for bonus turns, redesign to maximize wall length, and take money only if nothing else helps. Every point matters in the final scoring round because it awards the highest values and scores three places deep.

summary.title: Denomination Management
bullets: Maintain low-value cards (1-4) across all 4 currencies
Low denominations combine more flexibly for exact change
A hand of 1s, 2s, 3s > a hand of 9s

summary.title: Defensive Tile Purchasing
bullets: Buy tiles to block opponents' majorities
Store in reserve if you can't place them
Denying 13 pts to an opponent > gaining a marginal tile for yourself

summary.title: Wall Optimization and Perimeter Planning
bullets: Build L-shape or U-shape for max perimeter, not compact squares
High-wall tiles on outer edges, no-wall tiles in interior
Wall bonus can reach 8-12 pts per scoring round

summary.title: Scoring Round Timing
bullets: 1st scoring card in deck's 2nd fifth, 2nd in 4th fifth
Before scoring: place reserve tiles → they count toward majorities
Worth slight overpay to clinch a majority right before scoring

summary.title: High-Value Building Type Strategy
bullets: Tower 1st-place totals: 6+13+21 = 40 — Pavilion: 1+8+16 = 25
Sweet spot: mid-range types (Gardens, Chambers) with less competition
Read the table — target types fewest opponents pursue

summary.title: The Reserve as a Rearrangement Tool
bullets: Hold 1-2 tiles in reserve for end-game optimization
Redesign to swap poorly placed tiles for better configurations
One well-timed redesign can add 3-4 wall segments

summary.title: Reading the Money Display

Information advantage enables better purchasing decisions

summary.title: Endgame Point Maximization
bullets: Priority: place reserve tiles → exact-change buys → redesign walls → take money
Count remaining bag tiles to estimate turns left
Final round awards highest values and scores 3 places deep

entries.definition: Blue building tile. Scoring: 1st round — 1 point (1st place). 2nd round — 8 (1st), 1 (2nd). 3rd round — 16 (1st), 8 (2nd), 1 (3rd). The lowest-scoring building type, but also the least contested.
Red building tile. Scoring: 1st round — 2 points (1st place). 2nd round — 9 (1st), 2 (2nd). 3rd round — 17 (1st), 9 (2nd), 2 (3rd). Slightly more valuable than Pavilions with moderate competition.
Brown building tile. Scoring: 1st round — 3 points (1st place). 2nd round — 10 (1st), 3 (2nd). 3rd round — 18 (1st), 10 (2nd), 3 (3rd). Mid-range building type with balanced risk and reward.
White building tile. Scoring: 1st round — 4 points (1st place). 2nd round — 11 (1st), 4 (2nd). 3rd round — 19 (1st), 11 (2nd), 4 (3rd). A strong mid-to-high value target.
Green building tile. Scoring: 1st round — 5 points (1st place). 2nd round — 12 (1st), 5 (2nd). 3rd round — 20 (1st), 12 (2nd), 5 (3rd). High value with manageable competition.

Purple building tile. Scoring: 1st round — 6 points (1st place). 2nd round — 13 (1st), 6 (2nd). 3rd round — 21 (1st), 13 (2nd), 6 (3rd). The highest-scoring building type but the most contested and rarest.
One of four currency types. Money cards come in values 1 through 9. Used to purchase building tiles from the Denars market slot only. Must match the market slot currency exactly.
One of four currency types. Money cards come in values 1 through 9. Used to purchase building tiles from the Dirham market slot only. Must match the market slot currency exactly.
One of four currency types. Money cards come in values 1 through 9. Used to purchase building tiles from the Ducats market slot only. Must match the market slot currency exactly.
One of four currency types. Money cards come in values 1 through 9. Used to purchase building tiles from the Gulden market slot only. Must match the market slot currency exactly.
One of three possible actions on your turn. Take one money card of any value from the display, or take multiple cards whose total value is 5 or less. Refill display from deck after taking.

One of three possible actions on your turn. Choose a tile from the market and pay at least its price using money cards matching that slot's currency. No change given — overpayment is lost. Exact change earns a bonus turn.

One of three possible actions on your turn. Do exactly one of: remove a tile from your Alhambra to your reserve, place a tile from your reserve into your Alhambra, or swap a tile between your Alhambra and reserve. All placement rules apply.

When you pay the exact price for a building tile with no overpayment, you immediately take another full turn. Bonus turns can chain — paying exact change on a bonus turn earns yet another turn. The most powerful mechanic in the game.
Triggered when the first scoring card is drawn from the money deck. Only first place scores for each of the six building types. Ties divide points rounded down. Wall bonus also scored.
Triggered when the second scoring card is drawn. First and second place score for each building type. Higher point values than round one. Wall bonus scored again.

Triggered when the bag empties and the market cannot fully refill. First, second, and third place all score for each building type. Highest point values in the game. Wall bonus scored. Total of all three rounds determines the winner.
Having more tiles of a building type than any other player. Majorities are counted at each scoring round. Ties divide the combined points of the tied positions, rounded down.
One edge of a tile that has a wall printed on it. Walls appear as thick dark lines on tile edges. Zero to three edges of any tile may have walls.

The longest continuous chain of wall segments along the outside perimeter of your Alhambra. Scored at every scoring round — each player earns points equal to their longest wall length. Internal walls do not count.
When placing a tile, wall edges must face other wall edges or empty space. Open edges must face other open edges. You may never place a tile so a wall faces an adjacent tile's open edge.
Each player's starting tile and the center of their Alhambra. Every tile placed must be reachable from the fountain by traveling through connected open edges. The fountain has no walls.

A personal holding area for purchased tiles not yet placed in your Alhambra. Tiles in the reserve do not score for building majorities or walls. Tiles can be moved between reserve and Alhambra via the redesign action.
A central board with four spaces, one per currency type. Each space holds one face-up building tile available for purchase. Refilled from the bag after each purchase.
Four face-up money cards next to the draw deck. Players take money from this display. Refilled from the deck after cards are taken.
